speaker: interviewer
text: Tape six-- Vera Eden, June 20, 1996. How long did you stay in the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed in the army most of the year, then I demobilized because I-- I wasn't fit enough for-- for as much as I thought I was. I lost a lot of weight and-- and so on. We left, and Israel was our way out. We were not-- we were-- we were not-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] going to become Israelis. And we were not Zionists. And it was not the world I wanted to live in. My husband had a promised post-graduate aeronautical course in England as soon as he could get there. So that was his chance. And we got to England after that year in Israel, and he then got into the post-graduate aeronautical course, which was one very unique in those days in the world. And he became a specialist in turbine engines. I lived in London. He was in college. And I found a job, though I did not speak English. And I was thrown to the wolves, as they say, into a clinic and, knowing the instrumentation and knowing enough, within three months, I was speaking feasible English, and I picked it up. And then from there, I went into a private practice and worked there. And then after that, when my husband-- then I moved out of London and was closer. We-- we then rented the house near Woburn Sands. And my husband continued his second year of the year of his post-graduate, and I worked part-time down the street because a dentist moved in there. I always found that I can work, which was great. And when we finished-- when he finished his graduation, we-- we had a motorbike for transportation in England. And we crossed the channel, and we went on a trip of Europe out of our savings because he worked during his holidays and so on. And we went on-- on a trip on the motorbikes through Switzerland and-- and Austria and came back to England. And we got a booking on the which was the maiden voyage of the Holland American line of a new ship saying thank you to Canada for-- for liberating Holland. And that's how we got to Canada. And we came to Canada in 1952 in August 17th or 19th.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now just briefly, you-- where did you live when you came to Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we came to Canada, we were-- my husband was coming to a job already offered to him in England. He worked with Orenda in Toronto, and I got a job here right away. And we stayed in Toronto. We lived on Fielding near Eglinton. And we stayed a year. I worked in the Medical Arts Building on Bloor. Then we-- my husband decided that he wants to go into the aviation field. And he applied in Air Canada, got a job in Air Canada, and we transferred after that year to Montreal. We stayed in Montreal for-- from '53 to '54, where my son was born. I worked there with an oral surgeon. And then my husband was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] placed in Winnipeg as the head of the turbine new engines, which were coming in, and he was the first one who introduced the-- the Viscount engines in the North American continent. And they had that test [? bit ?] in Winnipeg, and we were three and a half years in Winnipeg. And I was at home with my new baby and-- and-- and learning how to-- how to diaper and to learn everything-- what it's all about. After that, we were-- in the 1958 in February, we were transferred back to Montreal. And that's-- I was already pregnant, and that's where my daughter was born, again, in the same hospital-- the [INAUDIBLE]-- and she was born in '58.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your daughter's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughter's name is Linda Elizabeth. My son's name is William John.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was born in '54.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your husband's name-- I think we neglected to get that.

speaker: interviewee
text: John [INAUDIBLE], which is really the same, but that's how he changed it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you stayed in Montreal how long?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed in-- in Montreal till three years ago, which is 1993. And then we-- our third daughter was-- was born in Montreal in '61. We had a big house in-- in [INAUDIBLE]-- which was a-- a beautiful suburbia. And I was a mother full-time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your third daughter's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sandra Caron. And that-- I stayed home until 1973, when I found that maybe, just maybe, I should go back into-- into the world and be Vera Eden instead of mother of or wife of. And it-- I was very successful on that part because I got back into the dental field when it was completely taking a change. It was changing into a new era of four-handed dentistry, of hygiene, and everything, which was going. And I was right there at the right time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I really got in, and I worked with really fabulous dentists in Montreal on surgery, on-- on-- in-- in-- in bridgework, crowns, in-- in-- in all the new possible way there is. And I was lucky enough that I was able to pick the best of the lot, otherwise I wouldn't stay. And I enjoyed it. And I retired at the age of-- in '93 at the age of 63 after 20 years-- after my children. I was working with a feeling of I'm leaving it not because I've got to but because I've got to get out of Quebec. Right. Now, have you returned to Europe? Have you returned to Prague?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I have been to Europe. I went-- well, I have been to Europe several times. But on my 60th year-- in my 60th year, I decided that I was ready and I was going to go and see and real-- and go and see the camps. So we flew to Frankfurt, and we rented a car, and we went to see Theresienstadt. We went to Bergen-Belsen and even to Auschwitz. Theresienstadt-- this was straight after the Russians have left and Eastern Europe became western world. And to my greatest amazement, Theresienstadt was completely a ghost town, but nobody-- there were some people living there, but there was no commemoration of any kind that it was a ghetto. There was people who are living-- who were living there in 1990 who did not know what it was. And I was completely appalled with the absolute ignorance of what was going on-- how it was completely withdrawn from-- from statistics. I have heard that, since then-- and we are six years later-- that, in 1995, the Israeli government have protested, and now Theresienstadt has got commemoration there. And if I do go to the Czech Republic, I will go and see it myself because that interests me. In Bergen-Belsen, they commemorated it-- very, very dignified. They burned down the whole mess and put up mass graves, saying approximately how many people are in the mass graves. They put a big monument to the Jewish population who died there, and they do-- did-- did a huge commemoration wall to the ones who were not Jewish. And it does look dignified. It's got the museum in front. I wanted to see all these places because, when I walked out of there, I was scared, scared child and didn't know what hit me. Now, I walked in there ready to see it-- emotionally ready to see it-- and I feel that I wanted to walk out with some kind of emotional grown-up impression. And the impression of Bergen-Belsen, walking out of it, they did a great job of I have got trouble with--

speaker: interviewer
text: Commemoration.

speaker: interviewee
text: Commemoration-- they did. But that does not give any excuse to what happened there. At least I know my mother is there somewhere, and it looks dignified. But it doesn't take anything-- my-- my anger or my-- my feelings haven't changed because of it. It just looks dignified. That's all I can say.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you went back to Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I went back to Auschwitz, which is in Poland-- and of course, Poland being at the eastern part of Europe, which was just returning into the western part-- number one-- when we went to Auschwitz, I was not in Auschwitz. I was in Birkenau, and it-- and when we went to Auschwitz, I said, this is-- my husband accompanied me on this trip-- I said, this is not where I was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had to ask, and we had to ask, and would you believe that nobody knew? About the fifth person directed us to the right place, which we finally found. Yes, they left it in a complete mess, and I really do not know which is better-- the complete mess, which they left behind, or the dignified way Bergen-Belsen is. I haven't made up my mind on it completely. I think the world should actually see it in the mess because it is terrifying. Even though they burned everything down and everything, Auschwitz is still terrifying.

speaker: interviewee
text: They have very little commemoration there. They-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and it was Birkenau. They still have the rail there, and I-- this famous rail-- and the point where Mengele stood. And when I stood there thinking that this was the point where my life was being taken or given to a degree, I just thought it was a very awful thing to-- just look at it. It was ordinary place in the middle of nowhere. And it was so very important. So that was my return. I will never, ever go back to any of those places. I did my pilgrimage there on my own time with my own emotion and with my own-- own feelings. I got an invitation from the German government to-- to commemorate the-- the 50th anniversary of Bergen-Belsen's liberation. They were going to pay me the trip, and they were going to pay my accommodation plus staying another week in Germany. And I replied with the facts that I did my pilgrimage on my own. I do not need to recognize the 50th anniversary, but I am glad. I didn't say I thank them, by God, but I said I'm glad that the-- that the ceremony is taking place, and I am not coming. So we have come to the end of a long story.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything you wanted to add? Is there anything we-- I have neglected to ask you or that you have forgotten to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: I do not think so. I feel that people like us have been marked emotionally for life, regardless that we don't-- some show it, and some don't. But we have. Our feelings and our emotions and our priorities of life are quite different to a normal person, if you can call anybody normal these days, but that would be my-- my assumption. I want to say that, though my childhood was very short-- nine short years-- it had a huge, beautiful impact on me of love, tenderness, understanding, and memories. And I want to give this whole tape in memory of my mom and dad because they were terrific parents. They really were. And I tried to do and be as good a person as they taught me to be. And I hope that I have succeeded. And that is just about all I have got to say.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you for sharing with us today.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. This is a picture-- a family picture-- which must have been taken approximately in 1935. This is the only family picture in existence. It's-- on the left is my mother Francesca. On the right is my father Anthony. On the left, the little child, is me, Vera. And in the middle is my sister Eva. And it is taken in Prague in a park. I cannot tell you where exactly, but it's taken in a park. This is a-- a call-up for the transport to Theresienstadt. It came from the-- from the Germans, of course, but it is under the umbrella of the Jewish community in Prague. It was on the 30th of July of 1942, and my number was AAV755. And my name there was Vera Glaser. This picture is taken in 1990 in Auschwitz. It is a place where human ashes are just lying there, and I have taken, on that trip, consciously, a rose from Canada-- the rose is wilted-- but I put it there for my father so he knows that I am living by choice in Canada, being a proud Canadian, and being very happy that I had the opportunity and gave the opportunity to my children to be born in Canada as Canadian citizens. Now, this is a picture in Bergen-Belsen. It's Vera Eden putting a rose from Canada, which I have taken purposely, to commemorate my mother, who died in Bergen-Belsen. And this monument is a monument to the Jewish people who died in Bergen-Belsen. And therefore, I placed this wilted rose all the way from Canada on that particular spot, and I hope that my mother will know about it.
